title: FBI arrests 21-year-old who allegedly stole $200,000 with malware-infected Steam games and spent the money on traceable UberEats gift cards
summary: Alleged cybercriminals have to eat, too.
body: The FBI has arrested a 21-year-old Florida resident, identified as Zyaire Dontaevious Zamarion Wilkins, for allegedly distributing malware embedded in a series of Steam games to approximately 8,000 PCs. Arrested last week, Wilkins—alongside two unnamed co-conspirators—is accused of using the private information stolen from those infected computers to steal over $200,000 in cryptocurrency. According to criminal complaint filed by the FBI (via Techspot ), Wilkins and his associates allegedly encouraged social media users to download their malware-laden games on Discord, Telegram, X, and LinkedIn, while also employing chatbots to identify and prioritize "individuals with large cryptocurrency holdings." (Image credit: Future) The FBI doesn't name Steam in the document, but refers to the weaponized games as being "available for download on a popular digital distribution software company for videogames located in the Western District of Washington," and the games it says were used for distributing malware—Dashverse (2024), Lunara (2024), PirateFi (2025), Blockblasters (2025), and Lampy (2026)—had all been previously named in its March 2026 call for information from users who had downloa…

title: The FDA made a big reversal in its cyclosporiasis probe, but it still thinks iceberg lettuce is to blame
summary: The FDA's investigation into the source of the cyclosporiasis outbreak is causing confusion as companies and consumers alike navigate new updates.

title: Modern Warfare 4 beta dates set for August, and this year Game Pass subscribers get no special treatment
summary: Pre-order players get five extra days of playtime.
body: It's that time of year again: The next Call of Duty is on deck, and Activision's got the beta dates to prove it. Modern Warfare 4 won't hit the streets until late October, but the multiplayer beta begins a full two months ahead of time. As usual, Activision is holding two beta weekends: One for those who pre-order the game and one for everyone else. Weekend 1 (August 21-25): For those who pre-order Modern Warfare 4 on PC, PS5, or Xbox (no Switch 2 beta) Weekend 2 (August 28 to September 1): A proper public beta on all platforms (including Switch 2) Fairly expected for a CoD beta calendar, except for a few abnormal details, like how Switch 2 players will be left out of that first weekend (that version might be coming in a little hot compared to the others). There's also the fact that pre-order weekend is five days instead of four—further stretching the definition of "weekend." That means pre-order players can start the same day as the Call of Duty Next livestream and won't have to watch streamers enjoy a 24-hour headstart. Less surprising, but surely disappointing for many, is that Game Pass subscribers will not be entitled to early beta access this year. They were for both Black O…

title: Think gaming handhelds are too expensive? Ayaneo says 'hold my beer,' lists $5,000+ monster
summary: How much is too much?
body: Ayaneo said it was suspending pre-orders for its Next 2 gaming handheld earlier this year after rising storage costs and the ongoing memory crisis made it " no longer sustainable ." Well, it's now listed on Ayaneo's website—with an asking price for the top model of $5,299 . I'll wait for you to pick up your jaw. Back with us? Good. The top-end Ayaneo Next 2 comes with an AMD Ryzen AI Max+ 395 APU, which is a Strix Halo beastie that's damn expensive no matter the device you find it in. Storage wise, you get a 2 TB SSD. Sheesh, I know NAND prices are silly right now, but surely a single NVMe drive can't be entirely to blame. As for the RAM? Well it's... oh. Oh I see. The top spec model comes with, brace yourselves, 128 GB of LPDDR5x-8533 . Yep, that would account for the super-silly price tag. I know the the Next 2 is positioning itself to be the handheld to end all handhelds , but 128 GB of RAM? For what, exactly? It's a Windows-based machine, so I suppose you could get up to some AI-based tomfoolery with it. Other than that, though, I cannot see a world where a 128 GB gaming handheld makes any sense. (Image credit: Future) Of course, there are cheaper models available. The 64 GB v…

title: If you're looking for ideas on how to mod your RTX 4060, maybe remove its fans and slap a ridiculously big heatsink on it like this person did
summary: Or don't, I can't tell you want to do.
body: There are many things that I would do to my graphics card—undervolt it, treasure it, sing it lullabies—but I probably wouldn't slap a gigantic heatsink on it just to eliminate the fan. Don't get me wrong, passive cooling is nice, but I'm glad I don't have to DIY it myself, creating the monstrosity I've just seen over on Chinese video platform Bilibili (check the top comment). The mod, re-posted by FanlessTech and reported by Videocardz , is slapped on top of an RTX 4060 GPU, and according to Google's machine translation you can message the Bilibili user for the schematic if you so wish. Nice passive GPU project https://t.co/bugtxJi14j pic.twitter.com/X91gredDVy July 20, 2026 Judging by a photo of the parts sitting on some scales, the heatsink weighs 2.56 kg (5.64 lbs), which makes sense considering it absolutely dwarfs the RTX 4060 PCB it's fitted to. You're not looking at your usual GPU heatsink here, as these are some ginormous riveted fins that are very spread apart. The DIYer has the Frankenstein card socketed into a dinky little motherboard next to some RAM and another fanless cooler for the CPU. It makes quite the peculiar scene. If there were ever a relatively recent GPU to…

title: Streamer builds a battery powered PC with over 100 AAs, uses Hannah Montana Linux to benchmark their abomination
summary: The best of both worlds?
body: Streamer Uwos Lab built an entirely battery powered gaming PC over the course of a five-hour stream last week . The rules were simple: no laptops, and no lithium ion AA batteries. The build had to be a desktop that could play a game while only relying on battery power. The self-described 'Mad-scientist streamer' explained their plan simply enough, saying, "We're going to wire eight AA in series to get 12 volts. And then we're going to take 50 of those banks and wire them in parallel to get a high current, 12 volts, feed that into a DC to ATX power supply, and get our desktop online." A bit of quick and dirty maths initially suggested the streamer would need about 400 Alkaline AA batteries to pull this project off, but they ended up only wiring together 192 in total. These were split across three laser-cut wooden boxes. Each one contains 64 batteries, plus all the clips and contact pads needed to transmit power to the desktop, and then is wrapped in duct tape (via Tom's Hardware ). I'd say 'Now, that's mad science,' but we're not quite there yet. As for the PC itself, Uwos Lab didn't say much about its specs. However, we do know its based on an AM4 motherboard, and does not have a …

title: Turns out Todd Howard is a full-time Reddit lurker just like the rest of us, says we'd 'be surprised how many great ideas' he gets from the site
summary: One of us. One of us.
body: Well, it turns out that Bethesda's executive producer Todd Howard and I have at least two things in common—we're both roughly 5 feet 6 inches tall, and we're both chronic Reddit lurkers. Admittedly, I only have IMDB to go by when it comes to the height thing. Maybe it's wrong! But the Reddit thing is 100% true, because he went ahead and said it himself. Howard gave the folks of r/Games a surprise jumpscare last week, making only his second public comment on the site in the last three years. It was, unsurprisingly, under a post sharing Bethesda essentially vomiting out every single thing it's currently working on , which came shortly after the announcement of parent owner Xbox's catastrophic layoffs . "Hey Reddit. I'm a professional lurker and read your comments all the time. Many here do and we deeply appreciate the passion for our work," Howard wrote. "I know I don't really post, and we're usually very quiet. Today's info was an attempt at changing that and letting everyone know what we've been working on the past several years, and a look at what's coming. So know we're constantly listening and keep telling us how to be even better." Howard then goes on to say: "You'd be surpris…

title: Activision has confirmed the Call of Duty movie setting, and it's not what I'd have picked
summary: If we're adapting generic shooters into movies, at least make it Black Ops.
body: I'm not sure who thinks we need a Call of Duty movie, but it's officially a thing, and it's coming out on June 30, 2028 . It's " an authentic and exciting experience for longtime fans and newcomers alike ." While this tidbit was confirmed earlier in the year, Activision and Paramount gave us little more than that in the months since. The big question was whether it will be set in the Modern Warfare or Black Ops universe, or something more unexpected, like WW2, Advanced Warfare, or Infinite Warfare (although probably not that last one). The answer is it's going to be set in the Modern Warfare timeline, revealed with a very short teaser trailer at FanaticsFest over the weekend. With Modern Warfare 4 coming up this October, presumably a Black Ops game next year, and then another Modern Warfare game in 2028, it lines up. Director Peter Berg revealed today at @FanaticsFest the @CallOfDutyMovie is set in the Modern Warfare universe.In theaters June 30, 2028. pic.twitter.com/CpdFX7kI9z July 18, 2026 Even as far as Call of Duty goes, Modern Warfare is easily the most generic setting Activision and Paramount could have picked. It's basically already a pastiche of bog-standard military film…

title: Palworld is only growing more powerful, hitting nearly a million concurrents in its second weekend
summary: This is just what happens when you give pokémon guns.
body: Palworld continues to prove that it's more than a flash-in-the-pan novelty. The survival crafting romp with legally distinct pokémon just had its second weekend since it hit 1.0, following a couple of years in early access, and it's only getting more popular. The second weekend saw concurrents approaching a million on Steam (961,867, to be exact), growing by more than 100,000 players since the previous weekend, which had already been extremely busy. It's an impressive showing for the game's second weekend, though not necessarily all that surprising. After two years in early access, there were probably a lot of folk who'd relegated it to the depths of their Steam library and are only now digging it out again. While it's enjoying a lot of attention right now, the peak of its popularity continues to be the early access launch, which saw more than 2 million players jump into the game. I confess that I do not see the appeal—I saw all I needed to in the first days of early access, and unlike, say, Grounded and its sequel , it proved incapable of overcoming my survival-crafting malaise. Some of my colleagues, however, have been having a grand old time, and as a result they've been able t…

title: Hot swappable mouse batteries might be the hot new trend as it looks like Wallhack's first ever mouse will have them
summary: The company's first gaming mouse looks pretty lush.
body: Wallhack (previously SkyPad, if you weren't aware) has teased a gaming mouse that will be announced on July 24. This will be the company's first gaming mouse, having previously stuck to high-performance mouse pads, sleeves, and skates. There must just be something irresistible about hot swappable batteries. This mouse looks to have them, as from the teaser trailer, we see a separate battery zooming into place. This also matches what peripheral reviewer mel0nFPS noted about a previous image-only teaser , as they predicted a "hot-swap battery system (250-300 mAh)." Meet The M-001. More information on 2026.07.24 pic.twitter.com/lOdm8HAMxJ July 20, 2026 I first saw a hot swappable mouse at Gamescom last year, when I was granted an early look at the Glorious Model O3 . Unfortunately that mouse failed to massively impress me for other reasons when I reviewed it (mostly mediocre tracking over wireless and stiff competition at such a high price), but I did like the battery system. Since then, we've seen other companies are having a go at it. There's the AM Infinity Mouse , which has a recent PixArt PAW3950 sensor and weighs just 49 g. And there's the as-yet unreleased Steelseries Rival Pr…

title: Final Fantasy 14's attempt to take the MMO mobile is over before the world even gets the chance to see it
summary: A Realm Re-gone.
body: You might be surprised to hear that the critically acclaimed MMORPG Final Fantasy 14—I'll spare you the free trial copypasta—has a mobile version. Well, it does… though er, not for much longer. A little over a year after launching in China, Final Fantasy 14 mobile is shutting down and taking its unreleased global version down with it. The mobile MMO was originally announced in November 2024 , with Square Enix handing the reins to PUBG Mobile developer LightSpeed Studios. It wasn't a straight port of what we've been playing on our PCs for the last decade, mind. For starters, the story only included base A Realm Reborn—that's everything up to the end of 2.0 with The Ultimate Weapon. Quests, systems, and jobs had also been nipped and tucked for a more streamlined and mobile-friendly experience, as well as a handful of features from later expansions folded in. It was essentially a strange stitched-together version, but one that also came with cosmetics and quality-of-life features that had longtime FF14 players (myself included) longing for their introduction into the main game. (Image credit: Square Enix) Gorgeous and extravagant hairstyles, the way the mobile version handled glamour…

title: That Game of Thrones RTS is still coming, still very HBO, but no longer due out this year
summary: They're checking the stables for Tyrek Lannister.
body: Game of Thrones: War for Westeros—the upcoming RTS from PlaySide emerging from the blasted wreckage of HBO's once-water-cooler-dominating fantasy TV series—would like you to know it still exists and is still coming out. Just, you know, a little later than it originally had planned. War for Westeros originally broke cover at last year's Summer Game Fest , promising big RTS battles, tricksy alliance-forging between the many houses of Westeros, and "deeply asymmetric strategies, with signature heroes, armies, and mechanics forged from the brutal legacy of Westeros." It was very much a game based on the TV show, and not the more well-regarded but less recognisable book series—you could tell because Charles Dance did the narration and there were some uncanny CGI renditions of Kit Harington and Nikolaj Coster-Waldau. But it's been quiet since then. That is, until an update last week informed fans that "development has been progressing well" in that time, and provided a little more insight into what the game might actually feel like to play. You can check out the full post here , but to my eye it all looks like a perfectly cromulent RTS. You've got swarms of men in armour and hero units …

title: Sonic & All-Stars Racing Transformed review (2018)
summary: Sonic & All-Stars Racing Transformed review: 'I can’t express how happy I am that somebody decided to make this'
body: Special commentary on this classic PC Gamer re-review provided by: Need to Know Release date January 31, 2013 Expect to pay $20 Developer Sumo Digital Publisher Sega Reviewed on Core i7-6700K, 16GB RAM, GTX 980 Steam Deck Verified Link Steam I’m not sure anybody has ever had more fun making a game than Sumo Digital did when it made Sonic & All-Stars Racing Transformed. On paper, the brief from SEGA would have been as desperately mercenary as they come: make a copy of Nintendo’s monumentally popular Mario Kart, but with Sonic. Take Nintendo’s near-perfect party game series and smear Sega’s blue weirdo and his friends all over it. Sumo seems to have understood that it was never going to make something pristine with the materials it was given. SEGA found itself resolutely Mean Girls-ed by the end of the ’90s console wars, too scrappy to mean anything as a brand and personally victimised by Nintendo’s radiant coherence. As mascots go, Mario is a stranger proposition than Sonic—a portly plumber is a weird hero to invest in when you’re a child—but sheer craftsmanship overruled the rule of cool. Sonic found himself too inherently ’90s to hold onto that cool very long anyway, destined for…

title: What's the best summer-themed videogame track?
summary: Get your ukuleles and steel pan drums at the ready.
body: Summer is officially here, and as a British person it is the law of this nation for me to say it's a bit hot, init? My AC-less apartment is being held together by a box fan and a dream. I haven't had a proper night's sleep in weeks, and I fear I've forgotten how it feels to be cold. Critical Hit (Image credit: Future) Welcome to Critical Hit , where I (or someone else on the PC Gamer team) celebrate and lament all things videogame music, audio design, and the ways our favourite games make our ears tingle. But if there's any positives to come out of the scorching heat and never-ending sun, it's that all the Vitamin D seems to be the perfect catalyst for summer-themed videogame bangers. I feel like there's nothing a videogame loves more than stuffing weather-appropriate tracks into its scores. Is it a bit rainy in-game? Switch up the music to a softer arrangement. Dead of winter? Perfect opportunity to make the music all twinkly and a touch festive. Summer music is no different, often leaning on beachy or water vibes for that big sunshine feeling. Ukuleles and steel pan drums are rampant to capture that vibe—think Destiny Islands from the first Kingdom Hearts game, or Ginger Island …

title: 'GTA 6 needs this': This GTA 5 prison mod turns one of roleplay's most enduring staples into a game within a game
summary: With flavours of Shawshank , this player-made masterstroke is a world unto itself.
body: I still remember cursing aloud when I was first sent to prison in GTA 5 roleplay. You see, unlike in regular Grand Theft Auto—story mode or Online—getting arrested in RP has consequences . Sure, you face the same trigger-happy cops when you fall foul of the law, where you're pursued until either dead or in cuffs, and are then hit with the same 'Wasted' or 'Busted' mandates depending on the outcome of the chase. Assuming the latter transpires, however, you don't simply receive a fine and a reset wanted star meter—in the majority of GTA RP servers, you instead respawn in the Bolingbroke penitentiary. The magnitude of your crime determines the length of your stay as you pace around the recreation ground doing everything you can to avoid contact with anyone else, lest you find yourself punching lumps out of one another and extending your stay. Now, XmanStudios has introduced the whole ordeal to GTA 5 singleplayer by way of their mod, Jailbreak . "Jailbreak is a GTA 5 script that adds a prison sentence system to singleplayer," reads the mod's description. "If the player dies or gets arrested while wanted, they are transported to Bolingbroke Penitentiary and must serve a sentence based …

title: US day traders flock to ‘the most dangerous product in crypto’

title: 007: First Light was originally going to be less about James Bond and more of 'an ensemble piece', with 'all the 00s working together, as they do in Slovakia'
summary: "You had the different agents working together."
body: hen I played 007: First Light earlier this year, one element of it stood out to me as unusual—and that is the arc relating to Bond's fellow 00 trainees, Cressida Bright and Lennox Monroe. IO Interactive puts a huge amount of work into building these characters, successfully selling you on the inherently bizarre concept of James Bond having flatmates. Yet just as you warm to these characters, they vanish from the story, and after MI6's failed operation to eliminate the rogue 009 in Slovakia, Bond primarily teams up with his mentor, Greenway. All of this is well portrayed. But the cadence of it nonetheless struck me as odd while playing. It turns out there might be a reason for this. IO Interactive initially intended for First Light to be less squarely focused on Bond, while his pals would have played a much bigger role in the story. Speaking to Eurogamer , First Light's lead writer Michael Vogt explained how the game's first act came together. "During the training sequence, we make a false promise," Vogt said. "Basically, we're saying 'This is going to be an ensemble piece, all the 00s working together, as they do in Slovakia'. "And then we pull the rug out from under you when most…

title: Every new Pal in Palworld 1.0 and where to catch them
summary: A wise man once told me, "Gotta catch 'em all," so I did.
body: The Palworld 1.0 update adds 72 new pals—most of which you can catch, while the remainder at least adds an updated entry to your Palpedia. That's a big undertaking with the expanded Palpagos map and the new World Tree region to explore, and referencing the in-game map can be a real pain, but that's where I come in to hopefully make the process a bit easier. How to catch every new pal in Palworld 1.0 This list was made to highlight where you're most likely to encounter the new 1.0 pals for the first time, but some spawn in more than one region. Pals that pop up in multiple locations will have a note indicating other places you can find them, but are only listed once to keep the process simple. The level recommendations don't account for bosses in the region, so keep that in mind before you go barreling into their arena. Some zones are harder or easier to catch new pals in depending on terrain obstacles and gear, so take them as loose guidelines—not hard and fast rules. A few tips to start collecting all 72 new pals Make sure to catch five of every pal for bonus XP and a Mimog Effigy. Use the Pal Tamer's Glasses, Ring of Mercy, or a pal with Mercy Hit so wild pals don't drop below 1…

title: Stardew Valley comes to Magic: The Gathering and fans aren't taking some of the new artwork well
summary: "Look how they massacred my boy."
body: The limited releases of cards for Magic: The Gathering that are mostly reskins of existing cards with new art are called Secret Lair drops. They're infamous for selling out fast and being hard to get hold of at normal prices, and they're also infamous for having unusual themes. There's been a Monty Python one , a Dwarf Fortress one , and even a Garfield one . Next? Stardew Valley. ConcernedApe's quirky farm-life sim has a legion of dedicated fans who have built their own mimetic lore on top of the actual story of the game, like Overwatch stans interpreting and shipping characters in ways Blizzard didn't intend. Take Elliott, for instance, a sentimental writer who lives on the beach and is among Stardew Valley's romanceable cast. According to fan artists, he's a willowy bishonen boy. According to ConcernedApe's own art for the Mermaid's Pendant card, a reskin of the existing Wedding Ring card, he's a square-jawed Chad. Wizards of the Coast Wizards of the Coast Wizards of the Coast Wizards of the Coast Wizards of the Coast Wizards of the Coast Wizards of the Coast Wizards of the Coast Wizards of the Coast Wizards of the Coast Wizards of the Coast Wizards of the Coast Wizards of the …

title: Grab a free Steam game full of misery and decision-making for a limited time
summary: The Life and Suffering of Sir Brante seems like it lives up to its name.
body: In 2021, when The Life and Suffering of Sir Brante came out on Steam, our Shaun Prescott called it "a text heavy narrative RPG with a huge emphasis on gruelling decision making." Think of those decision-heavy simulations like Princess Maker, only in a grim-and-gritty fantasy setting that cranks up the George R. R. Martin dial to max. "Step into the harsh world of the Blessed Arknian Empire and experience the life of Sir Brante," as the announcement puts it, "a man born into a rigid society where every choice matters and every decision carries consequences. Shape your destiny through years of struggle, ambition, sacrifice, and suffering in this narrative RPG, where your actions determine not only your own fate but also the fate of those around you." The Life and Suffering of Sir Brante is free to keep on Steam until July 23 to promote the upcoming release of a new game in the same setting, The Life and Suffering of Prince Jerian , which casts you in the role of a crown prince, born to a life of political intrigue. A whole new flavor of suffering to enjoy. I'll be playing Sir Brante to consider it for inclusion in the toughest choices in PC games listicle. If you can think of anythi…

title: The toughest choices in PC games
summary: And what happens when you make them.
body: PC games have been foisting tricky decisions on players since back in 1985 when Ultima 4 made us navigate a moral compass with eight different spokes to prove we were Britannia's best boy. But the Big Decision Era really kicked off in 2003 when Knights of the Old Republic made us pick between the light side and the dark, and every videogame was stuffed with choices people could fill their blogs arguing about. (We all had a blog in 2003. It was the law.) Those obligatory Big Decisions didn't always come off as tricky as they were supposed to. Fable's cartoonish morality was more of a toy to play with than a series of dilemmas to wrestle with, and BioShock making you choose between being a child murderer and a child non-murderer wasn't really a choice at all. Especially since the good playthrough offered even more rewards, albeit slightly delayed. And let's not even talk about the daft ending choice of Far Cry 3. Other developers nailed it, though. BioWare and Telltale in particular made Big Decisions their calling card, and we kept coming back for more. While some didn't land, or didn't have the impactful consequences we were hoping for, they still felt difficult in the moment. Her…

title: Question time: How long have you been playing MMOs?
summary: A new age. Or an old one, depending.
body: Terminally Online (Image credit: Future) This is Terminally Online : PC Gamer's very own MMO column. Every other week, I'll be sharing my thoughts on the genre, interviewing fellow MMO-heads like me, taking a deep-dive into mechanics we've all taken for granted, and, occasionally, bringing in guest writers to talk about their MMO of choice. One prevailing bit of industry wisdom about MMOs is the idea that they're a little grey in the beard—and so are their players, too. We can see a little of that in design philosophies for the modern MMORPG, which have leant more towards seasonal structures over long-term grinds, alongside more stuff to do for "midcore" players, who might be decently competent, but lack the time for organised play. There's also the point that most popular MMOs themselves are old—World of Warcraft's past its 20th year, FF14 is past its 10th. Newer games, like Amazon's New World, don't last long. Even Guild Wars 2, which I could've sworn was spritely, came out in 2012. Where the hell does the time even go? But I'm curious as to how that translates to you, dear reader. I myself have been playing MMOs since middle school—and while I did indeed beg my parents for a Wo…

title: For years, survival games have convinced us that chopping down 400 trees is somehow part of the fantasy—Palworld proves them wrong
summary: With a little help from my Pals.
body: Survival games are obsessed with the grind. Need a better axe? You've got to spend an hour collecting stone. Want to build a bigger base? Time to manually ferry hundreds of logs from one storage chest to another. Somewhere along the line, the genre started confusing friction with depth. Play This Right Now Welcome to Play This Right Now, the site version of our weekly newsletter in which we celebrate a new game, update, or DLC that we think is well worth checking out. You can subscribe to the newsletter at the link if you want to get our thoughts about a new videogame happening direct to your inbox every Thursday. But there are only so many trees I'm prepared to punch, so when Palworld originally came to Steam's early access platform, I dismissed it as "Pokémon with guns", a novelty that happened to explode, but would undoubtedly fade into obscurity as people looked for the next survival game to dig into. With the massively successful 1.0 release , it seemed like it was finally time to give it a proper go. After spending some hours with Palworld, I've discovered the thing that makes it special has almost nothing to do with the Pokémon bits, but everything to do with the way its "P…

title: Valve reassures Team Fortress 2 players that it's still working on the Mann vs Machine update it announced last year: 'We promise we're making progress'
summary: "It's taking a lot longer to sort through the new content and changes than we'd originally anticipated."
body: I'd completely forgotten Valve is working on an update for Team Fortress 2 's cooperative Mann vs. Machine mode, though in my defence, it's been a long time since we last heard about it. The prospective patch was announced in June last year, via a blog post on TF2's official website that requested map and mission submissions from the community in time for this update—with a "fictional deadline" of August 27. After that deadline passed, a brief progress report was provided in September. But for the past ten months there's been radio silence. Now though, Valve has provided another snippet of info on the patch's status on its blog. "We wanted to give everyone an update on the update we already gave on the update we're working on, and here it is: We're still working on the MvM update." That isn't everything Valve had to say on the matter. But it is about half of it. Valve elaborated that "it's taking a lot longer to sort through the new content and changes that we'd originally planned, but we promise we're making progress. Thanks for being patient!" This tracks with the progress report Valve gave back in September, where it explained that its request for community contributions result…

title: Glass mouse pads haven't won me over but gamer sleeves are a different story
summary: Mock me if you must. My gamer sleeve and I aren't listening.
body: Jacob Fox, hardware writer (Image credit: Future) This week I've been: Trying out a new mouse (a Superlight clone) and finally sacking off CS2's cheater-ridden Premier matchmaking for FaceIt. I'm known around the PC Gamer streets as a bit of a mouse pad nerd. Though that's just because I take the surface my little rodent skates across seriously, as you should. If you're playing a game that requires good aim, a good pad can be just as important for your gameplay experience as high fps, up to a certain point. Naturally, then, I fell under the spell of glass mouse pads for a while. With glass, there's the promise of a very slippery glide, if that's your thing, but more important is the promise of consistency and longevity. Cloth pads collect dust and dirt that can't just be wiped away, and the fibres change over time. That's why pros switch them out so often . Glass pads aren't like this, though. They do have irregularities etched into their surface to help with friction and sensor tracking, but these don't wear down because they're harder than your mouse skates. Your skates, on the other hand, do wear down quicker, but these are easily replaceable, and a cheap pack of dot skates can…

title: We just awarded $500 to the PC gamer who sent us this wild Crimson Desert video, and next month it could be you
summary: We're giving away $500 in Amazon gift cards every month, so send in your best gaming clips for a chance to win.
body: Even a few months after it launched, we still haven't gotten tired of seeing the wild stuff you come up with in Crimson Desert's sprawling sandbox. That's why our latest monthly winner of PC Gamer Clips is yet another great Crimson Desert video—and that's why the person who sent us that clip just won $500 in Amazon gift cards. If this is the first you're hearing about PC Gamer Clips, it's a snap: just record yourself playing a PC game, send us the clip, and each month we'll pick our favorite and award its creator a $500 prize. Visit our PC Gamer Clips page to find the submission form and prize restrictions (you need to be a US or UK citizen to be eligible). Below you can check out our most recent winner demonstrating a pretty slick way to swap a horse for a wyvern: Pretty smooth moves… though I would have liked to have seen the dragonrider make a dive to catch the unfortunate horse they just leapt off. Did it just go splat at the bottom of the cliff? Ah, well, these things happen. That's not the only Crimson Desert clip we've received. Here, check out some more of our favorites: And we'd love to see clips from any of the great PC games you're playing these days: Marathon, Counter-…
